RAG (OpenAI),"What is the difference between public, protected, package-private and private in Java?","

In Java, are there clear rules on when to use each of access modifiers, namely the default (package private), public, protected and private, while making class and interface and dealing with inheritance?

,

The official tutorial may be of some use to you.

,In Java, access modifiers are used to set the visibility and accessibility of classes, methods, and variables. The four main access modifiers are `public`, `protected`, package-private (default), and `private`. Each modifier serves a specific purpose and is used in different scenarios to control access to the components of a class.

- A member declared as `public` is accessible from any other class, regardless of the package it belongs to. This means that the method or variable can be accessed from outside the class where it is defined.
Here, `value` can be accessed from any other class.
- **Usage**: Use `public` when you want to expose the member to all other classes. This is common for methods that are part of the public API of a class【6:0†source】【6:6†source】.
- A member declared as `private` is accessible only within the class it is defined in. It cannot be accessed from outside the class.
Here, `value` can only be accessed within the `Example` class.

- **Usage**: Use `private` to encapsulate the details of a class and prevent external classes from accessing and modifying the internal state directly. This is a key principle of object-oriented programming to ensure data integrity and hide implementation details【6:0†source】【6:7†source】【6:8†source】.
- A member declared as `protected` is accessible within its own package and by subclasses, even if they are in different packages.
Here, `value` can be accessed within the same package and by subclasses of `Example`.

- **Usage**: Use `protected` when you want to allow subclasses to access and possibly modify the member, but still want to restrict access from non-subclass classes outside the package. This is useful in inheritance hierarchies where subclasses need to use or override certain members of the parent class【6:3†source】【6:5†source】.
- If no access modifier is specified, the member is accessible only within its own package. This is the default access level.
Here, `value` can be accessed only within the same package.

- **Usage**: Use package-private when you want to restrict access to classes within the same package. This is useful for grouping related classes that need to interact closely but should not expose their internals to the entire application【6:0†source】【6:8†source】.
- **Public**: Use for methods and variables that need to be accessible from any other class.
- **Private**: Use for methods and variables that should not be accessible outside the class. This is the most restrictive access level and is often used to enforce encapsulation.

- **Protected**: Use for methods and variables that should be accessible to subclasses and classes within the same package. This is useful in inheritance to allow subclasses to use and extend the functionality of the parent class.
- **Package-Private**: Use for methods and variables that should be accessible only within the same package. This is the default access level and is useful for grouping related classes together.

By carefully choosing the appropriate access modifier, you can control the visibility and accessibility of your class members, which helps in maintaining a clean and robust codebase【6:0†source】【6:1†source】【6:3†source】【6:5†source】【6:7†source】【6:8†source】.",683,,,,,0.1578947368421052,0.6485668684300504,3,"['Access modifiers such as public and private are used to control access to members of a class .', 'that global variables and subroutines with no access modifier can be used anywhere in the same package as the class where they are defined , but not in other packages', 'When protected is applied as an access modifier to a method or member variable in a class , that member can be used in subclasses -- direct or indirect -- of the class in which it is defined , but it can not be used in non - subclasses .']","['In Java , access modifiers are used to set the visibility and accessibility of classes , methods , and variables .', 'for methods and variables that should be accessible to subclasses and classes within the same package', 'Each modifier serves a specific purpose and is used in different scenarios to control access to the components of a class .', 'Use ` protected ` when you want to allow subclasses to access and possibly modify the member , but still want to restrict access from non - subclass classes outside the package .']",150,6342,828,,,java,gpt-4o

What's the best way to get the current date/time in Java?
What's the best way to get the current date/time in Java?
It depends on what form of date / time you want:

• If you want the date / time as a single numeric value, then System.currentTimeMillis() gives you that, expressed as the number of milliseconds after the UNIX epoch (as a Java long). This value is a delta from a UTC time-point, and is independent of the local time-zone1.
• If you want the date / time in a form that allows you to access the components (year, month, etc) numerically, you could use one of the following:
• new Date() gives you a Date object initialized with the current date / time. The problem is that the Date API methods are mostly flawed ... and deprecated.

• Calendar.getInstance() gives you a Calendar object initialized with the current date / time, using the default Locale and TimeZone. Other overloads allow you to use a specific Locale and/or TimeZone. Calendar works ... but the APIs are still cumbersome.

• new org.joda.time.DateTime() gives you a Joda-time object initialized with the current date / time, using the default time zone and chronology. There are lots of other Joda alternatives ... too many to describe here. (But note that some people report that Joda time has performance issues.
• in Java 8, calling java.time.LocalDateTime.now() and java.time.ZonedDateTime.now() will give you representations2 for the current date / time.
Prior to Java 8, most people who know about these things recommended Joda-time as having (by far) the best Java APIs for doing things involving time point and duration calculations.
With Java 8 and later, the standard java.time package is recommended. Joda time is now considered "

,In Smalltalk, `self` and `yourself` serve distinct purposes within a method body, and understanding their differences is crucial for effective programming in this language.

`self` is a pseudo-variable that refers to the receiver of the current message. When a method is invoked on an object, `self` within that method refers to the object itself. It is used to send messages to the same object, often to call other methods within the same class or to access instance variables.
In this example, `self setup` within the `initialize` method sends the `setup` message to the same instance of `MyClass` that received the `initialize` message.

`yourself` is a method that simply returns the receiver. It is often used in method chaining to ensure that the final result of a series of message sends is the original object, not an intermediate result. This is particularly useful in scenarios where you want to configure an object through a series of messages and then return the object itself.
In this example, `^self yourself` ensures that the `configure` method returns the instance of `MyClass` after setting its properties, allowing for method chaining.
- **Purpose**: `self` is used to refer to the current object to send messages to it, while `yourself` is used to return the current object, often for method chaining.

- **Usage Context**: `self` is typically used within methods to call other methods or access instance variables. `yourself` is used at the end of a method to return the receiver, ensuring the object itself is returned rather than an intermediate result.

By understanding these differences, you can effectively use `self` and `yourself` to write clear and functional Smalltalk code.",398,,,,,0.125,0.5623062063229456,1,['* self always refers to the receiver of the currently executing method .'],['# # # ` self `   ` self ` is a pseudo - variable that refers to the receiver of the current message .'],91,1508,477,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-4o

,In Pharo, the `subclassResponsibility` method is used to indicate that a method must be implemented by subclasses. It serves as a placeholder in an abstract class, signaling that any concrete subclass must provide its own implementation of this method. This is a common practice in object-oriented programming to enforce a certain structure and ensure that subclasses adhere to a specific contract.

1. **Enforcing Implementation**: By using `subclassResponsibility`, you ensure that any subclass of `AbstractDictionary` must implement the `size` method. If a subclass does not implement this method, an error will be raised, making it clear that the subclass is incomplete.

2. **Abstract Methods**: `subclassResponsibility` is a way to define abstract methods in Pharo. Abstract methods are methods that are declared but not implemented in the abstract class. They must be implemented by any concrete subclass.
3. **Design Clarity**: It makes the design of your class hierarchy clear. Anyone reading your code will immediately understand that `size` is a method that must be provided by subclasses.

Using `^self size` in the `AbstractDictionary` class would create an infinite recursive call because it would call itself indefinitely. This is because `self` in this context refers to the instance of the class that is currently executing the method, and since `AbstractDictionary` does not provide an implementation of `size`, it would keep calling itself.

If `AbstractArrayDictionary` is also an abstract class, it makes sense to use `self subclassResponsibility` there as well. This indicates that any concrete subclass of `AbstractArrayDictionary` must implement the `size` method. ,If your Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, you can interrupt the execution and break out of the loop using the following steps:
- Press `Alt + .` (Alt key and period key) on your keyboard. This key combination is designed to interrupt the current process in Pharo.
- After pressing `Alt + .`, Pharo should open a debugger window. This window will show you the current state of the execution stack, including the methods that are being called repeatedly.

- In the debugger, you will see a stack trace of the methods that are being executed. Identify the methods involved in the infinite loop. The stack trace will help you understand the sequence of method calls that led to the infinite loop.
- In the debugger, you have several options to handle the infinite loop. You can:
- **Terminate the Process:** Click on the ""Terminate"" button to stop the execution of the current process. This will halt the infinite loop and allow you to regain control of your Pharo image.

- **Step Through the Code:** If you want to investigate further, you can use the ""Step"" buttons to step through the code line by line. This can help you understand the exact point where the loop is occurring.
- **Fix the Code:** If you identify the problematic code, you can edit it directly in the debugger. After making the necessary changes, you can resume execution to see if the issue is resolved.

- Once you have successfully terminated the infinite loop and fixed the code, make sure to save your Pharo image to avoid losing your work again. You can do this by selecting ""Save"" from the Pharo menu.
Suppose you have two methods, `methodA` and `methodB`, that call each other indefinitely:

""Add a condition to prevent infinite recursion""

5. Save your Pharo image to preserve your changes.

By following these steps, you can effectively break out of an infinite loop in Pharo and prevent loss of work.",485,,,,,0.2105263157894736,0.591401691653693,4,"['Often , you can fix the code directly in the debugger window and click Proceed to continue running the application .', 'In the upper part of the debugger window you can see the execution stack , showing all the active methods .', 'When you quit Pharo , you will normally save a snapshot that contains all of your objects .', 'The window that opens with the error message is the Pharo debugger .']","['This window will show you the current state of the execution stack , including the methods that are being called repeatedly .', 'If you identify the problematic code , you can edit it directly in the debugger .', '` , Pharo should open a debugger window .', '* * Analyze the Stack :* *      - In the debugger , you will see a stack trace of the methods that are being executed .', 'Save your Pharo image to preserve your changes .']",178,1431,629,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-4o

GenAI,"Why is Smalltalk sometimes called ""dynamically typed""?","

I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" and not ""untyped"" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.
There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?

,

""Dynamically typed"" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean ""dynamically checked"". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.
See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.

,Smalltalk is referred to as ""dynamically typed"" because it determines the types of variables at runtime rather than at compile time. This means that the type of a variable is not explicitly declared in the code, but rather inferred based on the value assigned to it during execution.
- In Smalltalk, you do not specify types for variables, parameters, or method returns in the code. For example, you simply write:
Here, `a` can hold an integer, a string, or any other type of object without any type declaration.

- Smalltalk performs type checking at runtime. When a message (method call) is sent to an object, the system checks whether the object can respond to that message. If it cannot, a runtime error occurs. For example:
a printString.  ""This works because 5 can respond to printString""
a printString.  ""This also works because 'Hello' can respond to printString""
a notARealMethod.  ""This will cause a runtime error""

- **Dynamically Typed**: This means that while types are not explicitly declared, they are still present and checked at runtime. The system keeps track of the types of objects and ensures that operations are valid for those types.
- **Untyped**: This would imply that the language does not consider types at all, which is not the case for Smalltalk. Smalltalk does consider types, but it does so dynamically.

- Internally, Smalltalk does maintain type information about objects. Each object knows its class, and this class information is used to determine what messages (methods) the object can respond to. For example:
Consider a method that adds two numbers:
You can call this method with different types of numbers:
result1 := self add: 5 to: 10.  ""Works with integers""
result2 := self add: 5.5 to: 10.2.  ""Works with floats""
The method `add:to:` does not care about the types of `a` and `b` at compile time. It only ensures that the `+` operation is valid for the given types at runtime.

Smalltalk is called ""dynamically typed"" because it defers type checking to runtime, allowing for greater flexibility and polymorphism. It is not ""untyped"" because types do exist and are enforced during execution, ensuring that objects can only perform operations that are valid for their types.",526,,,,,0.1052631578947368,0.5500930042716194,2,"['6.3 Every object is an instance of a class Every object has a class

,In Pharo, the terms ""instanceVariableNames"" and ""classVariableNames"" refer to different types of variables that are used to store data within a class and its instances. Here's a detailed explanation of each:

Instance variables are specific to each instance (object) of a class. When you create an object from a class, that object can have its own set of data stored in instance variables. These variables are defined in the class definition and are unique to each instance of the class.
In this example, `MyClass` has two instance variables: `name` and `age`. Each instance of `MyClass` will have its own `name` and `age` values.
Here, `person1` and `person2` are two different instances of `MyClass`, each with its own `name` and `age`.

Class variables, on the other hand, are shared among all instances of a class. They are defined at the class level and are used to store data that is common to all instances of the class. These variables are also defined in the class definition but are shared across all instances.
In this example, `MyClass` has a class variable named `sharedCounter`. This variable is shared among all instances of `MyClass`.
Here, `sharedCounter` is a class variable that can be accessed and modified by all instances of `MyClass`.
In this case, `sharedCounter` is incremented twice, and since it is a class variable, the value is shared among all instances of `MyClass`.
- **Instance Variables**: Specific to each instance of a class. Each object has its own copy of these variables.
- **Class Variables**: Shared among all instances of a class. There is only one copy of these variables, shared by all objects of the class.

Understanding the difference between these two types of variables is crucial for designing classes and managing data in an object-oriented programming environment like Pharo.",389,,,,,0.0769230769230769,0.6226032692556193,1,['Class variables : variables shared between instances and classes .'],"['These variables are also defined in the class definition but are shared across all instances .', 'Each object has its own copy of these variables .   - * * Class Variables * * : Shared among all instances of a class .']",150,1455,630,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-4o

GenAI,"Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?","

Background
In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context).

column3: is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

column1: • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?

,

One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows ""self"". By returning ""self"" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the ""self"" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning ""self"" is the most elegant thing to do.

,In Smalltalk, methods return ""self"" by default if no explicit return value is provided. This design choice is rooted in both philosophical and practical considerations, which contribute to the language's elegance and usability.

1. **Uniformity and Simplicity**: Smalltalk was designed with the goal of creating a simple and uniform language. By defaulting to return ""self,"" Smalltalk ensures that every method has a consistent and predictable return value. This uniformity simplifies the mental model for programmers, as they can always expect a method to return the receiver unless specified otherwise.

2. **Message Passing Paradigm**: Smalltalk is built around the concept of message passing. In this paradigm, objects communicate by sending and receiving messages. Returning ""self"" by default aligns with this model, as it allows the receiver to continue participating in further message exchanges seamlessly.

1. **Method Chaining**: One of the most significant practical benefits of returning ""self"" by default is the facilitation of method chaining. Method chaining allows multiple methods to be called in a single, fluent expression. For example:
In this example, `myMethod`, `anotherMethod`, and `yetAnotherMethod` can be called in succession because each method returns the receiver, allowing the next method to be invoked on the same instance.

2. **Enhanced Readability and Conciseness**: Method chaining can lead to more readable and concise code. Instead of breaking down operations into multiple statements, developers can express a sequence of actions in a single, coherent line. This reduces boilerplate code and makes the intent of the code clearer.

3. **Fluent Interfaces**: Returning ""self"" by default supports the creation of fluent interfaces, where methods are designed to be chained together in a natural and readable manner. Fluent interfaces are particularly useful in domain-specific languages (DSLs) and APIs, where readability and ease of use are paramount.
Consider a class representing a graphical object that can be manipulated through various transformations:

RAG,What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?,"

In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

,

The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.
meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.
The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.
Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
which is a little bit more verbose.
To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.
,In Smalltalk, the terms `self` and `yourself` are both used within method bodies, but they serve different purposes and have distinct meanings.

`self` is a pseudo-variable that represents the receiver of the message. It is similar to the keyword `this` in Java. When a method is called on an object, `self` refers to that object within the method body. The method lookup starts in the class of the receiver when `self` is used (Pharo By Example, p. 60, 103, 105). For instance, if you have a method `doSomething` in a class `MyClass`, and you call `anInstance doSomething`, within the `doSomething` method, `self` will refer to `anInstance`.
Here, `self anotherMethod` sends the message `anotherMethod` to the same instance that received the `doSomething` message.

`yourself` is a method that simply returns the receiver. It is often used in method chains to return the original receiver instead of the result of the last message sent in the chain. This is particularly useful in builder patterns or when configuring objects, as it allows the method chain to return the object being configured rather than an intermediate result (Pharo By Example, p. 103).
In this example, `yourself` ensures that `anObject` is assigned the instance of `MyClass` being configured, rather than the result of `setProperty2:`.

- **Purpose**: `self` is used to refer to the receiver of the current message, allowing you to send further messages to it. `yourself` is used to return the receiver, often at the end of a method chain.

- **Usage Context**: `self` is used within method bodies to refer to the object on which the method was called. `yourself` is typically used at the end of a method chain to return the original object.

In summary, `self` is a pseudo-variable representing the receiver of the message, used to send further messages to the same object. `yourself` is a method that returns the receiver, useful for maintaining the original object in method chains (Pharo By Example, p. 60, 103, 105).",346,,,,,0.2222222222222222,0.6346242538475043,2,"['* self always refers to the receiver of the currently executing method .', '* Method lookup follows the inheritance chain
column2: self sends are dynamic and start the method lookup in the class of the receiver , whereas su113 The Pharo object model per sends start the method lookup in the superclass of class in which the super send is written .']","['# # # ` self `   ` self ` is a pseudo - variable that represents the receiver of the message .', 'The method lookup starts in the class of the receiver when ` self ` is used ( Pharo By Example , p. 60 , 103 , 105 ) .']",133,1466,522,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-4o

RAG,Smalltalk initialization variables,"

In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.
How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?
Or is this bad practice and should I always do

,

The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

• We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
• We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
• You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.
There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.

,In Pharo Smalltalk, object initialization is typically handled through the `initialize` method, which is automatically called when an object is instantiated using `new`. This is different from languages like Java and C++ where parameters are often passed directly to constructors.
To achieve parameterized initialization in Pharo, you can define custom class-side methods that create and initialize the object with the desired parameters. Here’s how you can do it:
1. **Define the `initialize` method**: This method sets up the default state of the object. It is automatically called when `new` is sent to the class.
2. **Create a custom class-side method**: This method will handle the creation and initialization of the object with specific parameters.
For example, if you want to create a `Color` object with a specific name, you can do the following:
""Create a new Color instance with the given name""
- The `initialize` method sets a default value for the `name` instance variable.
- The class-side method `named:` creates a new instance of `Color` and sets its name using the `name:` method.
You can then create a `Color` object with a specific name like this:

This approach is preferred over directly setting instance variables after object creation because it encapsulates the initialization logic within the class, making the code cleaner and more maintainable (Pharo By Example, pp. 93, 111).

Using accessor methods with lazy initialization (using `ifNil:` checks) is generally discouraged unless you have a specific reason, such as delaying expensive initializations (Pharo By Example, p. 96). Lazy initialization is useful when the initialization is costly or when the object might not always need the initialized state immediately (Pharo By Example, p. 111).",341,,,,,0.2727272727272727,0.6612382895568234,3,"['The initialize method is automatically executed after an object is created in Pharo .', 'The rest of the method sets up the state of this object .', 'Although it may be tempting to instead initialize instance variables in their respective accessor methods ( using ifNil : checks ) , avoid this unless you have a good reason .']","['In Pharo Smalltalk , object initialization is typically handled through the ` initialize ` method , which is automatically called when an object is instantiated using ` new ` .', 'This method sets up the default state of the object .', 'Using accessor methods with lazy initialization ( using ` ifNil : ` checks ) is generally discouraged unless you have a specific reason , such as delaying expensive initializations ( Pharo By Example , p. 96 ) .']",179,1409,476,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-4o

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).
There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:

The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been
6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with ""toy"" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)

,To access and modify the instance variables of an object in Smalltalk, such as the `year` variable in the `Class1` object `start-time`, you need to use accessor methods. In Smalltalk, instance variables are private to the instance itself, and there is no direct language syntax to access another object's instance variables unless accessor methods are defined (Pharo By Example, p. 89).
Here is how you can achieve this:

1. **Define Accessor Methods in Class1**: First, you need to define getter and setter methods for the `year` instance variable in `Class1`. This allows other objects to access and modify the `year` variable.
2. **Use Accessor Methods in Class2**: Once the accessor methods are defined, you can use them in `Class2` to set the `year` variable of the `start-time` object.
In this example, `start-time year: 2012` calls the setter method `year:` on the `start-time` object, setting its `year` instance variable to 2012.

This approach ensures proper encapsulation and adheres to the object-oriented principles of Smalltalk, where instance variables are accessed and modified through methods rather than directly (Pharo By Example, pp. 89, 298-299).

By following these steps, you can effectively access and modify the `year` variable in the `Class1` object `start-time` from within `Class2`.",228,,,,,0.1666666666666666,0.556618290699976,1,"['In Pharo , all instance variables are private to the object that owns them , so the only way for another object to read or write those variables is through accessor methods like this one .']","[""In Smalltalk , instance variables are private to the instance itself , and there is no direct language syntax to access another object 's instance variables unless accessor methods are defined ( Pharo By Example , p. 89 ) ."", 'This approach ensures proper encapsulation and adheres to the object - oriented principles of Smalltalk , where instance variables are accessed and modified through methods rather than directly ( Pharo By Example , pp . 89 , 298 - 299 ) .']",218,1378,363,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-4o

The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).
So for a method that, in Java or a similar ""curly bracket language"", might look something like this:
you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:
making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a ""symbol"" in a global dictionary):
That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):
Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.
Getting back to your case, you had this:

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says ""Variable or expression expected"" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.
• #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected
• if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side

• think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well
column2: ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)

column1: ,To define a Pharo class method that takes three arguments, you need to use the correct syntax for keyword messages. In Pharo, keyword messages can take an arbitrary number of arguments, and each part of the method name is followed by a colon and an argument name (p. 63).
Your current method definition has a syntax error because it does not correctly follow the keyword message format. Here is the correct way to define a method with three arguments:
MethodName: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3
^ self var1: argValue1 var2: argValue2 var3: argValue3
- `MethodName: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3` is the method signature.
- `argValue1`, `argValue2`, and `argValue3` are the arguments passed to the method.
- The method body uses these arguments to perform its operations.
When you want to call this method, you should use the following syntax:
ClassName new MethodName: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3
This call sends the message `MethodName:arg2:arg3:` to an instance of `ClassName` with the specified arguments.
Here is an example to illustrate:
MyClass >> MethodName: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3 [
- The method `MethodName:arg2:arg3:` is defined to return an array containing the three arguments.
- An instance of `MyClass` is created, and the method is called with the arguments `1`, `2`, and `3`.
- The result is printed to the Transcript.

You can choose different design (using polymorphism, lookup, ...) but that is pretty much the same for any OO language (for Smalltalk in particular, refer to this Refactoring if-chains in Smalltalk without class explosion ).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own ""if"" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.
E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

This naturally depends on your particular domain, maybe in different case the wording would be different. E.g. in case of collections, there's col ifEmpty: [ ] ifNotEmpty: [ ], for nil there's ifNil:ifNotNil:, for detection detect:ifFound:ifNone:, for dictionaries at:ifPresent:ifAbsent:, etc.

,In Pharo, there is no special syntax for control constructs like `if`, `elseif`, and `else`. Instead, these constructs are expressed by sending messages to booleans with blocks as arguments. To handle multiple conditions, you can chain `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages.
Here is how you can write an `if elseif elseif` construct in Pharo:
ifTrue: [ ""Do something if x is less than 25"" ]
ifTrue: [ ""Do something if x is greater than 25"" ]
ifFalse: [ ""Do something else if none of the above conditions are met"" ]

In this example, the first condition checks if `x` is equal to 25. If true, it executes the corresponding block. If false, it proceeds to check if `x` is less than 25, and so on. This chaining of `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages allows you to handle multiple conditions sequentially.

This approach leverages the fact that conditionals in Pharo are expressed by sending messages like `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:`, or `ifTrue:ifFalse:` to the result of a boolean expression (Pharo By Example, pp. 66, 83, 195-196).
This evaluates the expression `17 * 13 > 220` and sends the appropriate message based on the result (Pharo By Example, p. 66).

By chaining these messages, you can effectively create an `if elseif elseif` structure in Pharo.",346,,,,,0.6,0.7003514318368486,6,"['4.6 Conditionals and loops in a nutshell Pharo offers no special syntax for control constructs .', 'Instead , these are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans , numbers and collections , with blocks as arguments .', 'Some conditionals Conditionals are expressed by sending one of the messages ifTrue : ,', 'Following this principle , the conditional branches of ifTrue : or ifTrue : ifFalse : require blocks .', 'If the block evaluates to true , execution continues .', 'For instance , instead of an if - then - else control structure , conditionals are expressed as messages ( such as ifTrue : ) sent to Boolean objects .']","['In Pharo , there is no special syntax for control constructs like ` if ` , ` elseif ` , and ` else ` .', 'Instead , these constructs are expressed by sending messages to booleans with blocks as arguments .', 'To handle multiple conditions , you can chain ` ifTrue : ifFalse : ` messages .', 'This chaining of ` ifTrue : ifFalse : ` messages allows you to handle multiple conditions sequentially .', 'If true , it executes the corresponding block .', 'This approach leverages the fact that conditionals in Pharo are expressed by sending messages like ` ifTrue : ` , ` ifFalse : ` , or ` ifTrue : ifFalse : ` to the result of a boolean expression ( Pharo By Example , pp . 66 , 83 , 195 - 196 ) .']",200,1396,408,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-4o

,In Pharo, the `self subclassResponsibility` method is used to define abstract methods within abstract classes. An abstract class is designed to be subclassed rather than instantiated directly, and it typically contains methods that are meant to be overridden by its subclasses. These methods are known as abstract methods.

When you define an abstract method in Pharo, you use the `self subclassResponsibility` expression within the method body. This serves as a placeholder, indicating that the method must be implemented by any concrete subclass. If a subclass does not override this method and it gets called, an exception will be raised, signaling that the subclass should have provided an implementation (Pharo By Example, pp. 97, 186-187).
For example, consider the `size` method in your `AbstractArrayDictionary` class:

This method indicates that any subclass of `AbstractArrayDictionary` must provide its own implementation of the `size` method. If a subclass does not implement this method and it is called, an error will be raised, clearly indicating which method was not overridden (Pharo By Example, p. 311).

Using `self subclassResponsibility` is crucial because it enforces the contract that subclasses must fulfill. It ensures that the abstract class remains incomplete and cannot be used directly without providing the necessary implementations in its subclasses. This is a common practice in object-oriented design to define a framework for behavior that subclasses must adhere to (Pharo By Example, pp. 97, 186-187).
In contrast, if you were to implement the `size` method in `AbstractDictionary` as follows:

This would create an infinite recursive call, as the method would keep calling itself without ever reaching a concrete implementation. This is why the `self subclassResponsibility` approach is necessary—it explicitly marks the method as abstract and ensures that subclasses provide the required behavior (Pharo By Example, pp. 97, 186-187).

Therefore, you should not remove the `size` method from `AbstractArrayDictionary` if it is meant to be an abstract method. Instead, ensure that all concrete subclasses like `SimpleDictionary` and `FastDictionary` provide their own implementations of the `size` method. This maintains the integrity of your class hierarchy and ensures that each class fulfills its intended role (Pharo By Example, pp. 97, 186-187).",350,,,,,0.5454545454545454,0.6753529447039472,6,"['Instead , by convention , the body of an abstract method consists of the expression self subclassResponsibility .', '* Return self subclassResponsibility to make a method abstract .', 'An abstract class is a class that exists to be subclassed , rather than to be instantiated .', 'The "" placeholder "" methods , those that the other methods assume to be ( re)defined are called abstract methods .', 'An abstract class is usually incomplete , in the sense that it does not define all of the methods that it uses .', 'This indicates that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method .']","['This is why the ` self subclassResponsibility ` approach is necessary -- it explicitly marks the method as abstract and ensures that subclasses provide the required behavior ( Pharo By Example , pp . 97 , 186 - 187 ) .', 'In Pharo , the ` self subclassResponsibility ` method is used to define abstract methods within abstract classes .', 'An abstract class is designed to be subclassed rather than instantiated directly , and it typically contains methods that are meant to be overridden by its subclasses .', 'These methods are known as abstract methods .', 'When you define an abstract method in Pharo , you use the ` self subclassResponsibility ` expression within the method body .', 'It ensures that the abstract class remains incomplete and can not be used directly without providing the necessary implementations in its subclasses .', 'This serves as a placeholder , indicating that the method must be implemented by any concrete subclass .']",643,948,512,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-4o

In my example name:email:phone: is the method's selector and userName, userEmail and userPhone are parameters. You can call this method as in your example. name and email are either class side on instance side variables depending on where the method is defined.

,To address your queries about the syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0, let's clarify the correct approach and concepts:
### 1. Correcting the Syntax for Class Methods
In Pharo, class methods are defined on the class side, and instance methods are defined on the instance side. Your method definition and invocation have some issues. Here’s how you can correct them:
To define a class method, you need to ensure it is on the class side. Here’s an example of how to define a class method `createNewUser:name:email:phone:`:
CreateUser class>>createNewUser: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
To call this class method, you should use the class name followed by the method name:
CreateUser createNewUser: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone

In Pharo, class methods are dynamically-dispatched methods, unlike static methods in Java. This means inheritance, overriding, and super-sends work for class methods in Pharo (p. 92). Class instance variables are private to the class and its subclasses, similar to instance variables but at the class level (p. 92).

Instance variables in Pharo can only be accessed within the instance methods of the class that defines them or its subclasses. To access these variables from outside, you need to define accessor methods (getters and setters) (p. 89).
Here’s how you can define and use accessor methods for instance variables:
To call these instance methods, you need to create an instance of the class first:
- Define on the class side: `CreateUser class>>createNewUser: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone`
- Call using: `CreateUser createNewUser: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone`
- Class methods in Pharo are dynamically-dispatched.
- Class instance variables are private to the class and its subclasses.
- Define accessor methods to access instance variables.
- Create an instance of the class to call instance methods.

By following these guidelines, you should be able to correctly define and use class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0.",411,,,,,0.3125,0.6331675720545716,5,"['Instance variables can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them , and also in the methods defined in its subclasses .', 'However , the uniformity of the Pharo object model ( where classes are just regular objects ) means that they are somewhat different : whereas Java static methods are really just statically - resolved procedures , Pharo class methods are dynamically - dispatched methods .', 'Instance variables Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself .', ""This means that inheritance , overriding and super - sends work for class methods in Pharo , whereas they do n't work for static methods in Java ."", 'Class variables are private variables shared by the class and all the instances of the class .']","['Accessing Instance Variables    Instance variables in Pharo can only be accessed within the instance methods of the class that defines them or its subclasses .', 'Understanding Class and Instance Variables    In Pharo , class methods are dynamically - dispatched methods , unlike static methods in Java .', '* * Class and Instance Variables * * :      - Class methods in Pharo are dynamically - dispatched .      - Class instance variables are private to the class and its subclasses .', 'This means inheritance , overriding , and super - sends work for class methods in Pharo ( p. 92 ) .', 'Class instance variables are private to the class and its subclasses , similar to instance variables but at the class level ( p. 92 ) .']",522,1078,667,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-4o

RAG,"If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?","

Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!
I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?

,

Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.
If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the
• „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
• open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.

,If your Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, you can break out of the loop by using the keyboard shortcut `CMD-.` (Command and period) to interrupt the execution and open a debugger on the computation (Pharo By Example, p. 140). This will allow you to inspect the state of the program and identify the cause of the infinite loop.

In case the `CMD-.` shortcut does not work, you can bring up an emergency debugger by typing `CMD-SHIFT-.` (Command, Shift, and period) (Pharo By Example, p. 140). This emergency debugger can help you regain control of the image and stop the problematic process.

Once you have the debugger open, you can examine the call stack and identify the methods involved in the infinite loop. You can then terminate the offending process or edit the code to fix the issue. For example, you might insert a `self halt` statement in the suspect method to bring up the pre-debugger, allowing you to proceed or debug further (Pharo By Example, p. 140).

Additionally, it is good practice to save your work frequently. Pharo allows you to save the state of your image, so if you encounter an issue, you can restart from the last saved state without losing significant progress (Pharo By Example, p. 15).
Here is an example of how you might edit a method to include a halt for debugging purposes:
""Some code that might cause an infinite loop""
By including `self halt`, you can trigger the debugger at a specific point in your code, making it easier to diagnose and fix issues (Pharo By Example, p. 311).

In summary, use `CMD-.` to interrupt an infinite loop and open the debugger, or `CMD-SHIFT-.` for an emergency debugger. Save your image frequently to avoid losing work, and consider using `self halt` in your code to facilitate debugging (Pharo By Example, pp. 15, 140, 311).",321,,,,,0.5,0.6805218237373601,5,"['If you execute code that goes into an infinite loop , you can interrupt it and open a debugger on the computation by typing CMD- .', 'To set a breakpoint in a method , simply insert the expression send self halt at some point in the body of the method .', '( It is also useful to know that you can bring up an emergency debugger at any time by typing CMD - SHIFT- .)', 'When this message is sent , execution will be interrupted and a debugger will open to this point in your program ( see Chapter : The Pharo Environment for more details about the debugger ) .', '* When you open a Pharo image , you will find yourself in exactly the same state ( i.e. , with exactly the same running objects ) that you had when you last saved that image .']","['If your Pharo image goes into an infinite loop , you can break out of the loop by using the keyboard shortcut ` CMD- . `', 'In summary , use ` CMD- . ` to interrupt an infinite loop and open the debugger , or ` CMD - SHIFT- . ` for an emergency debugger .', 'For example , you might insert a ` self halt ` statement in the suspect method to bring up the pre - debugger , allowing you to proceed or debug further ( Pharo By Example , p. 140 ) .', 'In case the ` CMD- . ` shortcut does not work , you can bring up an emergency debugger by typing ` CMD - SHIFT- . `', '( Command and period ) to interrupt the execution and open a debugger on the computation ( Pharo By Example , p. 140 ) .', 'Pharo allows you to save the state of your image , so if you encounter an issue , you can restart from the last saved state without losing significant progress ( Pharo By Example , p. 15 ) .']",220,1389,434,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-4o

,To generate getter and setter methods automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak, you can leverage the reflective capabilities of Smalltalk. This involves dynamically adding instance variables and their corresponding accessors to a class. Here’s a step-by-step approach to achieve this:
1. **Define a Method to Add Instance Variables and Accessors:**
You can create a method that takes a class and a list of instance variable names, then dynamically adds these variables to the class and generates the corresponding getter and setter methods.
In Pharo, you can modify class definitions dynamically. This involves using the reflective methods provided by the system to add instance variables and compile new methods.
Here’s an example of how you might implement such a method:
aClass compile: varName, ': anObject', String cr, varName, ' := anObject.'
This method iterates over the list of variable names, adds each as an instance variable to the class, and compiles the corresponding getter and setter methods.
You can call this method with your class and list of instance variables:

Pharo also supports dynamic accessor generation using the `doesNotUnderstand:` mechanism. This can be useful if you want accessors to be created on-the-fly when they are first accessed. Here’s an example:

This method traps any message not understood by the object. If the message corresponds to an instance variable, it dynamically compiles a getter method for that variable and then re-sends the message to itself (pp. 315-316).

By following these steps, you can efficiently generate getter and setter methods for a class in Pharo or Squeak, ensuring that your code remains clean and maintainable while leveraging the powerful reflective capabilities of Smalltalk (pp. 49, 119-120, 298-299, 315-316).",555,,,,,0.4,0.6834319908111223,4,"['Let us look at a simple variant of this technique where we have a class that automatically adds accessors for its instance variables on demand : DynamicAcccessors > > doesNotUnderstand :', ""( self class instVarNames includes : messageName ) ifTrue : [ self class compile : messageName , String cr , ' ^ ' , messageName .]"", 'aMessage Any message not understood is trapped here .', 'If an instance variable with the same name as the message sent exists , then we ask our class to compile an accessor for that instance variables and we re - send the message .']","['` ` `       This method traps any message not understood by the object .', '* * Dynamic Accessor Generation :* *      Pharo also supports dynamic accessor generation using the ` doesNotUnderstand : ` mechanism .', ""Here 's an example :       ` ` ` smalltalk      DynamicAccessors > >"", ""aMessage          | messageName |          messageName : = aMessage selector asString . RAG,"Why is Smalltalk sometimes called ""dynamically typed""?","

I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" and not ""untyped"" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.
There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?

,

""Dynamically typed"" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean ""dynamically checked"". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.
See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.

,Smalltalk, and by extension Pharo, is referred to as a ""dynamically typed"" language because it does not require explicit type annotations for variables, parameters, or method returns. Instead, types are associated with objects at runtime rather than at compile time. This means that the type of an object is determined dynamically when the program is executed, rather than being statically checked by the compiler before execution (Pharo By Example, pp. 1, 100-101).

In Pharo, nearly all operations are performed by sending messages to objects. For instance, when you perform an operation like `3 + 4`, you are sending the `+` message with the argument `4` to the integer object `3`. The actual method that gets executed depends on the class of the object receiving the message (Pharo By Example, p. 100). This dynamic message sending is a core aspect of Pharo's dynamic typing system.

Moreover, variable declarations in Pharo are not executable statements but merely allocate space for object references. Assignments bind variable names to objects within their scope, and returns simply pass the computed result back to the caller. These operations do not involve static type checks (Pharo By Example, pp. 100-101).

The dynamic nature of Pharo's type system allows for a flexible and interactive programming environment, often referred to as ""live coding,"" where code can be modified and executed on the fly without the need for recompilation (Pharo By Example, p. 1).

In summary, Smalltalk is called ""dynamically typed"" because it determines the types of objects at runtime through dynamic message sending and does not require static type annotations, allowing for a highly flexible and interactive coding experience (Pharo By Example, pp. 1, 100-101).",272,,,,,0.4545454545454545,0.6928819106685622,5,"['In Pharo there are only dynamic message sends .', 'Ok , so nearly everything in Pharo happens by sending messages .', 'A live coding environment that allows the programmer to modify code while the code executes , without any need to slow down their workflow .', 'In fact , variable declarations are not even executable .', 'One of the unique features of Pharo ( and its ancestors ) is that while you are programming , you are living in a world of live objects , not in a world of static program text .']","['In Pharo , nearly all operations are performed by sending messages to objects .', 'live coding , "" where code can be modified and executed on the fly without the need for recompilation ( Pharo By Example , p. 1 )""', ""This dynamic message sending is a core aspect of Pharo 's dynamic typing system ."", 'Moreover , variable declarations in Pharo are not executable statements but merely allocate space for object references .', 'The dynamic nature of Pharo \'s type system allows for a flexible and interactive programming environment , often referred to as "" live coding , "" where code can be modified and executed on the fly without the need for recompilation ( Pharo By Example , p. 1 ) .']",224,1377,366,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-4o

,In Pharo, ""instanceVariableNames"" and ""classVariableNames"" serve different purposes and have distinct scopes within a class definition.

Instance variables are specific to each instance of a class. They are defined using the ""instanceVariableNames"" keyword in the class definition. These variables are private to the instance itself, meaning that each instance of the class has its own set of values for these variables, and they cannot be directly accessed by other instances of the same class. This encapsulation ensures that the internal state of an object is protected and can only be accessed or modified through methods defined in the class or its subclasses (p. 89).
For example, if you define a class `Point` with instance variables `x` and `y`:
Each `Point` object will have its own `x` and `y` values, and these values can be accessed within the instance methods of the `Point` class or its subclasses.

Class variables, defined using the ""classVariableNames"" keyword, are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself. This means that there is only one copy of each class variable, and it is accessible to all instances of the class as well as the class methods. Class variables are useful for storing data that needs to be shared across all instances, such as configuration settings or counters (pp. 109-110).
For example, if you define a class `Dog` with a class variable `count` to keep track of the number of `Dog` instances:
The `count` variable will be shared among all `Dog` instances and can be accessed and modified by class methods to keep track of the total number of `Dog` objects created.

- Class variables are shared among all instances of the class and the class itself, making them accessible in both instance and class methods (pp. 109-110).
- Instance variables provide encapsulation at the instance level, ensuring that the internal state of an object is protected from other instances (p. 89).

- Instance variables are used to store data specific to each instance.

RAG,"Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?","

Background
In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context).

column3: is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

column1: • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?

,

One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows ""self"". By returning ""self"" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the ""self"" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning ""self"" is the most elegant thing to do.
,In Smalltalk, methods return ""self"" by default if no explicit return value is specified. This design choice is rooted in the philosophy and practical benefits of the language.

The default return of ""self"" aligns with the principle that methods should only return a value when it is of interest to the sender. Kent Beck refers to this as the ""Interesting return value"" principle: ""Return a value only when you intend for the sender to use the value"" (Pharo By Example, p. 104). This approach encourages clarity in code, making it explicit when a method's return value is meant to be used.

1. **Method Chaining**: One of the primary practical benefits of returning ""self"" by default is to facilitate method chaining. Method chaining allows multiple methods to be called in a single statement on the same object, enhancing readability and conciseness. For example:
Here, each method call returns the receiver, allowing the next method to be called on the same object seamlessly (Pharo By Example, p. 103).

2. **Consistency and Simplicity**: By having methods return ""self"" by default, Smalltalk maintains a consistent and simple model where every method call results in an object being returned. This uniformity simplifies the mental model for developers, as they can always expect an object to be returned, even if it's just the receiver itself (Pharo By Example, p. 103).

3. **Encapsulation and Message Passing**: Smalltalk's design emphasizes message passing and encapsulation. Since instance variables are private and can only be accessed through messages, returning ""self"" by default supports this encapsulation by ensuring that methods can be chained without exposing internal state directly (Pharo By Example, p. 101).
The result of `myInstance myMethod` is `myInstance` itself, allowing further messages to be sent to `myInstance` in a chained manner (Pharo By Example, p. 103).

In summary, methods in Smalltalk return ""self"" by default to support method chaining, maintain consistency, and adhere to the principle of returning values only when they are of interest. This design choice enhances the readability, simplicity, and encapsulation of Smalltalk code.",369,,,,,0.125,0.6218536372237411,2,"['This is a common idiom in Pharo , which Kent Beck refers to as Interesting return value :', '"" Return a value only when you intend for the sender to use the value . ""']","['Kent Beck refers to this as the "" Interesting return value "" principle : "" Return a value only when you intend for the sender to use the value "" ( Pharo By Example , p. 104 ) .', 'Return a value only when you intend for the sender to use the value "" ( Pharo By Example , p. 104 )""']",301,1303,535,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-4o

The `Set` class in Pharo is designed to behave as a mathematical set, meaning it does not allow duplicate elements (Pharo By Example, pp. 207-208). ,Choosing between Squeak and Pharo for a beginning Smalltalker, especially for someone with a background in Ruby, can be nuanced. Both Squeak and Pharo are modern implementations of Smalltalk-80, but they have diverged in their focus and community support.

1. **Modern and Actively Developed**: Pharo started as a fork of Squeak in March 2008 and has since evolved with a focus on modern software development practices. The current version is Pharo 5.0, with Pharo 6.0 in alpha development as of 2017 (p. 2).

2. **Live Coding Environment**: Pharo supports live coding, allowing programmers to modify code while it executes, which can be particularly appealing for those who enjoy interactive development (p. 1).
3. **Powerful IDE**: Pharo provides a robust Integrated Development Environment (IDE) with tools to manage complex code and promote good design (p. 1).

4. **Rich Library and Portability**: Pharo includes a rich library and a very fast Just-In-Time (JIT) compiling virtual machine. It is highly portable, running on OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, iOS, and Raspberry Pi (p. 2).
5. **Community and Culture**: Pharo has a welcoming community that values changes and improvements, making it a supportive environment for newcomers (p. 1).
6. **Seaside Framework**: Pharo serves as the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework, which is a powerful tool for building web applications (p. 2, p. 260).

1. **Educational and Multimedia Focus**: Squeak has a strong emphasis on educational and multimedia applications. It was originally developed with a focus on directness and liveness, making it a good choice for interactive and graphical applications (p. 235).

2. **Historical Significance**: Squeak has a rich history and has been used as a platform for many innovative projects. It retains many of the original ideas from the Self programming language, emphasizing direct manipulation of objects on the screen (p. 235).
- Modern development environment with active updates.
- Strong support for professional software development and research.
- Rich ecosystem and powerful tools for web development with Seaside.
- Highly portable and easy to debug and analyze due to its self-contained virtual machine (pp. 1-2).
- May have a steeper learning curve for those unfamiliar with its extensive tools and libraries.
- Excellent for educational purposes and multimedia projects.
- Emphasizes direct manipulation and liveness, which can be very intuitive for beginners (p. 235).
- May not have the same level of modern development tools and community support as Pharo.

For a Rubyist starting with Smalltalk, **Pharo** is likely the better choice due to its modern development environment, active community, and strong support for web development with Seaside. It offers a robust platform for both professional and research purposes, making it a versatile tool for various projects (pp. 1-2, p. 260). However, if your interest lies more in educational or multimedia applications, **Squeak** might be worth exploring due to its historical focus and intuitive interface (p. 235).",486,,,,,0.4,0.6698788522803184,6,"['The Pharo project started in March 2008 as a fork of Squeak ( a modern implementation of Smalltalk-80 ) , and the first 1.0 beta version was released on July 31 , 2009 .', 'Pharo comes with the Cog VM a very fast JITing VM .', 'The current version is Pharo 5.0 , released in May 2016 .', 'The Pharo community is friendly and active .', 'Pharo is a modern , open - source , dynamically typed language supporting live coding inspired by Smalltalk .', 'Pharo serves as the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework available at http://www.seaside.st .']","['Pharo includes a rich library and a very fast Just - In - Time ( JIT ) compiling virtual machine .', 'The current version is Pharo 5.0 , with Pharo 6.0 in alpha development as of 2017 ( p. 2 ) .', 'Pharo supports live coding , allowing programmers to modify code while it executes , which can be particularly appealing for those who enjoy interactive development ( p. 1 ) .', 'Pharo has a welcoming community that values changes and improvements , making it a supportive environment for newcomers ( p. 1 ) .', '* * Modern and Actively Developed * * : Pharo started as a fork of Squeak in March 2008 and has since evolved with a focus on modern software development practices .', 'Pharo serves as the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework , which is a powerful tool for building web applications ( p. 2 , p. 260 ) .']",196,5556,724,5.0,"['What is Pharo?', 'What is the easiest way to get started with Seaside?', 'What should be done to start Pharo?', 'What is Pharo an example of?', 'What is Pharo for?']",pharo,gpt-4o

,In Smalltalk, the terms `self` and `yourself` are used within method bodies but serve different purposes.

`self` is a pseudo-variable that always refers to the receiver of the currently executing method. It is similar to the keyword `this` in Java. When a message is sent to `self`, the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver (Pharo By Example, p. 60, 103, 105). For example, if you have a method `foo` in class `A` and you call `self bar` within `foo`, the system will look for the method `bar` starting from class `A`.
If `A` has a subclass `B` and an instance of `B` receives the message `foo`, `self` within `foo` will refer to the instance of `B`.

`yourself` is a method that simply returns the receiver. It is often used in method chains to ensure that the final result of the chain is the receiver itself, rather than an intermediate result. This is particularly useful in scenarios where you want to return the object that received the message after performing a series of operations on it (Pharo By Example, p. 103).
For example, consider the following method chain:
Here, `yourself` ensures that the final result of the chain is `anObject`, not the result of `doThat`.
To illustrate the difference, consider the following example:
- `self doSomething` sends the message `doSomething` to the receiver of `initialize`, which is an instance of `MyObject`.
- `self yourself` returns the receiver of `initialize`, which is also the instance of `MyObject`.

In summary, `self` is used to refer to the receiver of the current method and is essential for message sending within the object's context. `yourself`, on the other hand, is used to return the receiver itself, often to maintain the receiver as the result of a method chain.",354,,,,,0.2,0.633677878291372,2,"['* self always refers to the receiver of the currently executing method .', ""This means that all messages sent to self are looked up by starting in the receiver 's class .""]","['# # # ` self `   ` self ` is a pseudo - variable that always refers to the receiver of the currently executing method .', ""In summary , ` self ` is used to refer to the receiver of the current method and is essential for message sending within the object 's context ."", 'When a message is sent to ` self ` , the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver ( Pharo By Example , p. 60 , 103 , 105 ) .']",133,5498,495,5.0,"['What is the difference between writing Pharo object models as self new and WebServer new?', 'When is the pseudovariable self used in Pharo?', 'What does Self should do in the context of SUnit testing?', 'What are self and super in this context?', 'What is the difference between super bindings and self?']",pharo,gpt-4o

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

• We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).

,In Pharo Smalltalk, object initialization and parameter passing to constructors are handled differently compared to languages like Java and C++. Instead of passing parameters directly to a constructor, Pharo uses a combination of instance creation and initialization methods.

To create and initialize an object with specific parameters, you typically define an `initialize` method in the class. This method is automatically called when a new instance is created using the `new` message. If you need to pass parameters, you can define a custom class-side method that creates the instance and then sets the necessary properties.
For example, consider a `Color` class where you want to initialize a color with a specific name:
2. **Define a custom class-side method to handle parameterized initialization**:
