This approach ensures that the object is properly initialized and encapsulates the initialization logic within the class itself, promoting better design and maintainability (Pharo By Example, pp. 89, 93).

Avoid directly setting instance variables outside the class, as it breaks encapsulation. Instead, use accessor methods to set and get instance variables. This practice aligns with the encapsulation principles in Pharo, where instance variables are private to the instance itself and can only be accessed through methods defined in the class or its subclasses (Pharo By Example, p. 89).

In summary, to initialize objects with parameters in Pharo Smalltalk, define custom class-side methods that create and initialize instances, ensuring encapsulation and proper object-oriented design.",362,,,,,0.1818181818181818,0.6436255336708059,2,"['Some developers prefer to access instance variables only through accessors .', 'Instance variables Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself .']","['Instead , use accessor methods to set and get instance variables .', 'This practice aligns with the encapsulation principles in Pharo , where instance variables are private to the instance itself and can only be accessed through methods defined in the class or its subclasses ( Pharo By Example , p. 89 ) .']",179,5755,412,5.0,"['What are instance variables in Pharo?', 'What are class variables in the Pharo object model?', 'What are the most generic ways to create instances of collections in Smalltalk?', 'What is the example given for accessing a global object in Smalltalk globals?', 'What should one do to define a global in Smalltalk?']",pharo,gpt-4o

RAG+CoI,For loop for array in Pharo Smalltalk,"

I'm trying to make an array with random numbers (just 0 or 1), but when I run it, it just prints this: End of statement list encountered ->
1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
1. The method selector doesn't start with a lowercase letter
It is a tradition for selectors to start with a lowercase letter. In this sense, genLista would be more correct than GenLista.
2. The method selector includes the abbreviated word 'gen'
For instance, genLista could be renamed to genereLista o listaAlAzar (if you decide to use Spanish)
The result of Array new: 31 is an array of 31 elements. However, the code below it only fills 30 of them, leaving the last one uninitialized (i.e., nil). Possible solution: lista := Array new: 30.
4. A dot is missing causing a compilation error
<pre><code>  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).
There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:
the missing dot will generate the runtime error self not understood by block.
5. There is a more expressive way of generating 0s and 1s at random
The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been
6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with ""toy"" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)

,To create an array with random numbers (0 or 1) in Pharo Smalltalk, you can use the `to:do:` loop to iterate over the indices of the array and populate it with random values. Your initial code is almost correct, but there are a few adjustments needed to ensure it works properly.
Here is the corrected version of your code:
1 to: 31 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: (2 atRandom - 1) ].
This line creates a new array of size 31. In Pharo, arrays are fixed-size collections, and the first element is at position 1, not 0 (p. 203).
1 to: 31 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: (2 atRandom - 1) ].

This line iterates from 1 to 31 (inclusive) and for each index `i`, it puts a random number (either 0 or 1) at the position `i` in the array. The expression `(2 atRandom - 1)` generates a random number between 0 and 1. The `to:do:` construct is used to iterate over a range of numbers, where `1 to: 31` specifies the range and `do:` takes a block that is executed for each number in the range (p. 67).
The `^` symbol is used to return the value from the method (p. 62).
This will create an array of 31 elements, each being either 0 or 1, and print it to the Transcript.
- **Element Access**: The methods `at:` and `at:put:` are used to access and modify elements in an array (p. 203).
- **Iterating Over Collections**: The `do:` method is a basic collection iterator that applies a block to each element of the receiver (p. 213).

By following these guidelines, you can effectively create and manipulate arrays in Pharo Smalltalk.",350,,,,,0.25,0.6291680354022624,3,"['Contrary to the C convention in Pharo , the first element of an array is at position 1 and not 0 .', 'It applies its argument ( a block taking a single argument ) to each element of the receiver .', 'The main protocol to access array elements is the method at : and at : put : .']","['In Pharo , arrays are fixed - size collections , and the first element is at position 1 , not 0', 'a basic collection iterator that applies a block to each element of the receiver ( p. 213 )', 'The methods ` at : ` and ` at : put : ` are used to access and modify elements in an array ( p. 203 ) .   - * *']",197,7638,535,5.0,"['What is Pharo Smalltalk?', 'Which control constructs are typically expressed in this manner in Pharo?', ""What methods are used to access and put elements in Pharo's arrays?"", 'What are the most generic ways to create instances of collections in Smalltalk?', 'What is ""OrderedCollection withAll"" in Pharo?']",pharo,gpt-4o

,To access and modify instance variables in Smalltalk, such as the `year` variable in the `Class1` object `start-time`, you need to use accessor methods. In Pharo, instance variables are private to the instance itself, meaning that they cannot be accessed directly from outside the instance (p. 89). Therefore, you must define accessor methods within the class to get and set these variables.
Here is how you can achieve this:
You need to define getter and setter methods for the `year` variable in `Class1`. This allows other objects to access and modify the `year` variable.
These methods allow you to read (`year`) and write (`year:`) the `year` variable from outside the instance.
In the `initialize` method of `Class2`, you can now set the `year` variable of the `start-time` instance of `Class1` using the setter method.
This code creates new instances of `Class1` for `start-time` and `end-time`, and then sets the `year` of `start-time` to 2012 using the `year:` method.

By following these steps, you ensure that the encapsulation principles of Pharo are respected, and you can safely access and modify instance variables through well-defined interfaces (pp. 89, 298-299).",271,,,,,0.1428571428571428,0.6151918016600323,1,['Instance variables Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself .'],"['In Pharo , instance variables are private to the instance itself , meaning that they can not be accessed directly from outside the instance ( p. 89 ) .']",218,5884,372,5.0,"['What are instance variables in Pharo?', 'What are class variables in the Pharo object model?', 'What are several ways to find a class in Pharo?', 'What is the example given for accessing a global object in Smalltalk globals?', 'What should one do to define a global in Smalltalk?']",pharo,gpt-4o

What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :

,

The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).
So for a method that, in Java or a similar ""curly bracket language"", might look something like this:
you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:
making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a ""symbol"" in a global dictionary):
That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):
Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.
Getting back to your case, you had this:

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says ""Variable or expression expected"" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.
• #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected
• if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side

• think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well
column2: ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)

column1: ,To define a Pharo class method that takes three arguments, you need to use the correct syntax for keyword messages. In Pharo, keyword messages can take multiple arguments, and each part of the message name is followed by a colon and an argument. Here is the correct way to define such a method:
MethodName: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3
^ self var1: argValue1 var2: argValue2 var3: argValue3

In this method definition, `MethodName:arg2:arg3:` is the full name of the method, and it takes three arguments: `argValue1`, `argValue2`, and `argValue3`. Each part of the method name is followed by a colon and the corresponding argument.
When you want to call this method, you should use the same keyword message syntax:
ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: argValue2 var3: argValue3
This will send the message `var1:var2:var3:` to the `ClassName` object with the specified arguments.
Here is an example to illustrate this:
MyClass class >> methodName: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3 [
^ self var1: argValue1 var2: argValue2 var3: argValue3

In this example, `MyClass` is a subclass of `Object`, and it defines a class method `methodName:arg2:arg3:`. The method returns the result of sending the message `var1:var2:var3:` to `self` with the provided arguments.

Remember, in Pharo, methods are defined in the context of a class, and the syntax for defining methods and sending messages is designed to be readable and expressive (Pharo By Example, pp. 59-64). The method will return `self` by default if no explicit return value is provided (Pharo By Example, p. 103).",268,,,,,0.0,0.6145754923343325,0,[],[],217,7920,455,5.0,"['How does Pharo function?', 'What are the Pharo instance variables in this context?', 'What is Pharo Syntax?', ""What is Pharo's model of message sending?"", 'What does a method in Pharo return by default?']",pharo,gpt-4o

column1: RAG+CoI,SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript,"<pre><code>|oc|
Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:

,

Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

You can choose different design (using polymorphism, lookup, ...) but that is pretty much the same for any OO language (for Smalltalk in particular, refer to this Refactoring if-chains in Smalltalk without class explosion ).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own ""if"" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.
E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

This naturally depends on your particular domain, maybe in different case the wording would be different. E.g. in case of collections, there's col ifEmpty: [ ] ifNotEmpty: [ ], for nil there's ifNil:ifNotNil:, for detection detect:ifFound:ifNone:, for dictionaries at:ifPresent:ifAbsent:, etc.
,In Pharo, control constructs such as `if`, `elseif`, and `else` are expressed by sending messages to Boolean objects with blocks as arguments. Pharo does not have special syntax for these constructs
column2: instead, it uses message sends to achieve conditional logic (Pharo By Example, p. 66).

column1: To handle multiple conditions, you can chain `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages. Here is an example of how you can check three conditions using `ifTrue:ifFalse:` in Pharo:
ifTrue: [ ""Do something if x is less than 25"" ]
ifTrue: [ ""Do something if x is greater than 25"" ]
ifFalse: [ ""Do something if none of the above conditions are met"" ]

In this example, each condition is evaluated in sequence. If the first condition `(x = 25)` is true, the corresponding block is executed. If it is false, the next condition `(x < 25)` is evaluated, and so on. This chaining of `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages allows you to handle multiple conditions in a clear and structured manner (Pharo By Example, pp. 83, 195-196).

Remember that in Pharo, both the receiver and the argument of the `ifTrue:ifFalse:` message must be blocks, as blocks are objects that can be executed multiple times depending on the context (Pharo By Example, p. 83). This approach leverages the object-oriented nature of Pharo, where the Boolean objects themselves determine which block to execute based on their value (Pharo By Example, p. 196).",352,,,,,0.5,0.6892306286115621,4,"['For instance , instead of an if - then - else control structure , conditionals are expressed as messages ( such as ifTrue : ) sent to Boolean objects .', 'Following this principle , the conditional branches of ifTrue : or ifTrue : ifFalse : require blocks .', '4.6 Conditionals and loops in a nutshell Pharo offers no special syntax for control constructs .', 'Some conditionals Conditionals are expressed by sending one of the messages ifTrue : ,']","['In Pharo , control constructs such as ` if ` , ` elseif ` , and ` else ` are expressed by sending messages to Boolean objects with blocks as arguments .', 'that in Pharo , both the receiver and the argument of the ` ifTrue : ifFalse : ` message must be blocks , as blocks are objects that can be executed multiple times depending on the context ( Pharo By Example , p. 83 )', 'This chaining of ` ifTrue : ifFalse : ` messages allows you to handle multiple conditions in a clear and structured manner ( Pharo By Example , pp . 83 , 195 - 196 ) .', 'Pharo does not have special syntax for these constructs

This will then call self with at:put:duplicate: . This method is implemented by the 3 afore mentioned classes, and then the self which is pointing to the instance of the class that made the initial call is sent the message to execute at:put:duplicate:.

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows
Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.

you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.
Now in general about what you should know.

• You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.

• You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
<li>It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
• when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).

• when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error ""message not understood by MagicBagDictionary"".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement
in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:
yes, you can get my size, but I have no idea how to calculate it because I'm not complete. But my subclasses should know that, so don't hesitate to ask.

,In Pharo, the `subclassResponsibility` method is used to define abstract methods within abstract classes. An abstract class is designed to be subclassed rather than instantiated directly, and it typically contains methods that are meant to be overridden by its subclasses. These methods are placeholders, indicating that the subclass must provide a concrete implementation.

When you define a method with `self subclassResponsibility`, you are signaling that this method must be implemented by any subclass. If a subclass does not override this method and it gets called, an exception will be raised, indicating that the subclass should have provided an implementation. This is a convention in Pharo to ensure that certain methods are always overridden in subclasses (Pharo By Example, pp. 97, 186-187).
For example, consider the `size` method in your `AbstractArrayDictionary` class:

This method indicates that any subclass of `AbstractArrayDictionary` must implement the `size` method. If a subclass does not provide an implementation and the method is called, an error will be raised, clearly stating that the subclass should have overridden the method (Pharo By Example, p. 187).

Using `self subclassResponsibility` is crucial for maintaining a clear contract between the abstract class and its subclasses. It ensures that subclasses provide specific behavior that the abstract class relies on. This is why you should not implement the `size` method in `AbstractDictionary` as:

This implementation would cause an infinite loop because it calls itself recursively without ever reaching a concrete implementation. Instead, by using `self subclassResponsibility`, you enforce that each subclass must provide its own implementation of `size`, thus avoiding such issues (Pharo By Example, p. 311).

In summary, `self subclassResponsibility` is a way to define abstract methods in Pharo, ensuring that subclasses provide necessary implementations. This helps in maintaining a clear and enforceable contract within the class hierarchy, preventing runtime errors due to missing method implementations (Pharo By Example, pp. 97, 186-187, 311).",309,,,,,0.3333333333333333,0.6470186721613532,4,"['* Return self subclassResponsibility to make a method abstract .', 'The "" placeholder "" methods , those that the other methods assume to be ( re)defined are called abstract methods .', 'An abstract class is a class that exists to be subclassed , rather than to be instantiated .', 'SubclassResponsibility signalFor : thisContext sender selector By convention , methods that send self subclassResponsibility are considered to be abstract .']","['In Pharo , the ` subclassResponsibility ` method is used to define abstract methods within abstract classes .', 'These methods are placeholders , indicating that the subclass must provide a concrete implementation .', 'An abstract class is designed to be subclassed rather than instantiated directly , and it typically contains methods that are meant to be overridden by its subclasses .', 'When you define a method with ` self subclassResponsibility ` , you are signaling that this method must be implemented by any subclass .', 'In summary , ` self subclassResponsibility ` is a way to define abstract methods in Pharo , ensuring that subclasses provide necessary implementations .', 'Using ` self subclassResponsibility ` is crucial for maintaining a clear contract between the abstract class and its subclasses .']",643,5290,446,5.0,"['Why are methods that send self subclassResponsibility abstract by convention?', 'When is the pseudovariable self used in Pharo?', 'What is Object>>subclassResponsibility?', 'By what convention are methods that send self subclassResponsibility considered?', 'What does Self should do in the context of SUnit testing?']",pharo,gpt-4o

I guess you misunderstand the method syntax a bit, because createNewUser:Arguments part makes no sense. What you should have is a method on the class side like this:

In my example name:email:phone: is the method's selector and userName, userEmail and userPhone are parameters. You can call this method as in your example. name and email are either class side on instance side variables depending on where the method is defined.

Also you shouldn't name a class CreateUser. Think about this, what will be the instances called? ""createUsers""? Usually you name a class User, then you can think about instances as ""users"", and then the responsibility of the class object is ""to create users (its instances)"".

2) In Pharo (and Smalltalk) the concept is a bit simpler. ,To address your queries about the syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0, let's break down the issues and provide the correct approach.
### 1. Correcting the Syntax for Class and Instance Methods

In Pharo, class methods are defined on the class side and are dynamically-dispatched, unlike static methods in Java which are statically-resolved procedures (p. 92). Here is how you should define and call a class method:
CreateUser class>>createNewUserWithName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone

Instance methods are defined on the instance side and are used to manipulate instance variables. To create an instance and call its methods, you use the `new` keyword to instantiate the class and then send messages to the instance.
CreateUser>>initializeWithName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
### 2. Concept of Static vs. Class Variables/Methods

In Pharo, class methods are similar to static methods in Java, but with dynamic dispatch capabilities (p. 92). Class variables in Pharo are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself, similar to static variables in Java (pp. 109-110). However, Pharo also has class instance variables, which are private to the class and its subclasses, not shared like static variables in Java (p. 92).

Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself and can only be accessed directly within the instance methods of the class that defines them or through accessor methods (p. 89). To access these variables, you should define getter and setter methods.
1. **Class Methods**: Define on the class side using `class>>methodName` and call using `ClassName methodName`.
2. **Instance Methods**: Define on the instance side and call using an instance created with `new`.
3. **Static vs. Class Variables/Methods**: Class methods in Pharo are dynamically-dispatched, and class variables are shared among instances and the class itself.
4. **Accessing Instance Variables**: Use accessor methods to get and set instance variables.

By following these guidelines, you should be able to correctly define and use class and instance methods and variables in Pharo 4.0.",400,,,,,0.3571428571428571,0.6382524374410065,5,"['Instance variables Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself .', 'However , the uniformity of the Pharo object model ( where classes are just regular objects ) means that they are somewhat different : whereas Java static methods are really just statically - RAG+CoI,"If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?","

Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!
I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?

,

Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.
If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the
• „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
• open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.

,If your Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, you can break out of it by using the keyboard shortcut `CMD-.` (Command + period) to interrupt the running process and open a debugger on the computation (PBE, p. 140). This allows you to inspect the state of the program and terminate the problematic process.

Once you have interrupted the process, you can use the debugger to examine the execution stack and the state of various objects. The debugger provides tools to step through code, inspect objects, and even modify code on the fly (PBE, pp. 133-134, 50). This can be particularly useful for diagnosing and fixing the issue that caused the infinite loop.
If the debugger itself is unresponsive or if you need to bring up an emergency debugger, you can use the keyboard shortcut `CMD-SHIFT-.` (Command + Shift + period) (PBE, p. 140).

In case you need to kill the infinite loop from the process viewer, you can use the World Menu to access the process browser. From there, you can identify and terminate the offending process. This can be done by selecting the process and using the available options to halt or terminate it.

To prevent losing your work in the future, it's a good practice to save your image frequently. Pharo allows you to save a snapshot of your current state, which includes all objects and code changes. You can do this by selecting ""Save and quit"" or ""Quit"" from the World Menu, which will prompt you to save your image (PBE, pp. 14-15).

If you do lose work due to a crash or an infinite loop, you can recover your changes from the `.changes` file. This file logs all the changes to the source code. You can use the ""Recover lost changes"" option from the World Menu to browse recent changes and restore your work (PBE, pp. 156-157).
Here is a summary of the steps to break out of an infinite loop and recover your work:
1. **Interrupt the Process**: Use `CMD-.` to interrupt the running process and open the debugger (PBE, p. 140).
2. **Use the Debugger**: Inspect the execution stack and state of objects, and terminate or fix the problematic code (PBE, pp. 133-134, 50).
3. **Emergency Debugger**: If needed, use `CMD-SHIFT-.` to bring up an emergency debugger (PBE, p. 140).
4. **Process Viewer**: Access the process browser from the World Menu to identify and terminate the offending process.
5. **Save Frequently**: Regularly save your image to avoid losing work (PBE, pp. 14-15).
6. **Recover Changes**: Use the ""Recover lost changes"" option to restore work from the `.changes` file (PBE, pp. 156-157).

By following these steps, you can effectively manage and recover from infinite loops in Pharo.",440,,,,,0.4375,0.6729491724476604,7,"['If you execute code that goes into an infinite loop , you can interrupt it and open a debugger on the computation by typing CMD- .', 'Using the debugger , you can execute code step by step , inspect objects in parameters and local variables , evaluate code just as you can in a playground , and , most surprisingly to those used to other debuggers , change the code while it is being debugged !', 'Additionally you can use the World Menu and select either Save and quit or Quit .', 'The debugger showing the execution stack and the state of various objects .', '( It is also useful to know that you can bring up an emergency debugger at any time by typing CMD - column1: ,To create a class in Pharo Smalltalk, you typically use the System Browser, which is the central development tool in Pharo. Here’s a step-by-step guide based on the information from ""Pharo By Example"":
1. **Open the System Browser**: Navigate to the System Browser from the World menu.

2. **Select a Package**: In the left-most pane, select the package where you want to create your new class. If you need to create a new package, right-click in the package pane and select ""Add package..."" (pp. 119-120).

3. **Use the Class Creation Template**: When you click on the selected package, the bottom browser pane will display a class creation template. This template is a Pharo expression that sends a message to an existing class (usually `Object`), asking it to create a subclass (pp. 119-120). For example:

4. **Edit the Template**: Replace `Object` with the superclass you want to inherit from (usually `Object` or one of its subclasses), replace `#Dog` with the name of your new class, and fill in the instance variable names if you know them (pp. 119-120).
5. **Accept the Changes**: Either right-click and select ""Accept"" or use the shortcut CMD-s (for ""Save""). This will compile the new class definition (p. 36).

6. **Define Class and Instance Methods**: Once the class is created, you can define methods for it. Methods on the instance side are those that will be available to instances of the class, while methods on the class side are available to the class itself (pp. 91-92). For example, to define a class method to initialize a class variable:
And to define an instance method:

7. **Class and Instance Variables**: Instance variables are private to the instance itself and can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses (p. 89). Class instance variables are private to the class and are not shared with subclasses (p. 92).
8. **Testing the Class**: You can test your class in a Playground or Transcript. For example:

In summary, creating a class in Pharo involves using the System Browser to edit a class creation template, defining instance and class methods, and understanding the encapsulation of instance and class variables. ,In Pharo, you can dynamically generate getter and setter methods (accessors) for a class using a combination of reflection and meta-programming techniques. Here’s a detailed explanation of how you can achieve this:

Pharo allows you to dynamically add methods to a class, including getter and setter methods for instance variables. This can be particularly useful when you have a long list of instance variables, such as those derived from an existing SQL database.
You can define a new class and its instance variables using the class creation template in the System Browser. For example:
You can use the `doesNotUnderstand:` method to dynamically generate accessors when they are first called. Here’s an example of how you can implement this:
self class compile: messageName, String cr, ' ^ ', messageName.

This method traps any message not understood by the object. If the message corresponds to an instance variable, it dynamically compiles a getter method for that variable and then resends the message to itself (p. 315).

To automate the creation of accessors for a list of instance variables, you can define a method that takes a class and a list of variable names, and generates the corresponding getter and setter methods:
addVariablesAndAccessors className: MyClass variablesList: #(aaaa bbbb cccc)
MyClass compile: varName, String cr, ' ^ ', varName.
MyClass compile: varName, ': anObject', String cr, varName, ' := anObject.'].
This method iterates over the list of variable names, compiling both getter and setter methods for each variable (p. 315).
Here’s how you can use the above method to add variables and accessors to a class:
addVariablesAndAccessors className: MyClass variablesList: #(aaaa bbbb cccc).
This will result in the creation of getter and setter methods for the instance variables `aaaa`, `bbbb`, and `cccc` in the class `MyClass`.
- **Encapsulation:** In Pharo, instance variables are private to the object that owns them. Access to these variables should be provided through accessor methods (p. 49, p. 89).
- **Readability:** Pharo emphasizes code readability. Ensure that dynamically generated methods are well-documented and follow naming conventions (p. 36).

By following these steps, you can efficiently generate getter and setter methods for a class in Pharo, making your code more maintainable and adhering to best practices in object-oriented programming.",582,,,,,0.4615384615384615,0.7034207744646694,6,"['Let us look at a simple variant of this technique where we have a class that automatically adds accessors for its instance variables on demand : DynamicAcccessors > > doesNotUnderstand :', 'These rules make Pharo code as easy to read as possible .', 'Instance variables Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself .', 'aMessage Any message not understood is trapped here .', ""( self class instVarNames includes : messageName ) ifTrue : [ self class compile : messageName , String cr , ' ^ ' , messageName .]"", 'If an instance variable with the same name as the message sent exists , then we ask our class to compile an accessor for that instance variables and we re - send the message .']","['You can use the ` doesNotUnderstand : ` method to dynamically generate accessors when they are first called .', 'an example of how you can implement this :      ` ` ` smalltalk      DynamicAccessors', 'Pharo emphasizes code readability .', 'In Pharo , instance variables are private to the object that owns them .', ""Here 's a detailed explanation of how you can achieve this :    # # # Dynamic Generation of Accessors    Pharo allows you to dynamically add methods to a class , including getter and setter methods for instance variables ."", '` ` `      This method traps any message not understood by the object .', ""aMessage          | messageName |          messageName : = aMessage selector asString .          ( self class instVarNames includes : messageName )              ifTrue : [                  self class compile : messageName , String cr , ' ^ ' , messageName .                  ^]"", 'If the message corresponds to an instance variable , it dynamically compiles a getter method for that variable and then resends the message to itself ( p. 315 ) .']",373,5261,715,5.0,"['What is Pharo?', 'What is required to create a new class or method in the Pharo environment?', 'How is the code in Pharo described?', 'According to Pharo convention, what is the name of a getter method?', 'How can one define instance variables and methods in Pharo?']",pharo,gpt-4o

The issue with your initial code is that it forks a new process for each iteration, causing all the delays to run in parallel and thus printing all messages at once after 2 seconds.

- `[i < 5] whileTrue:` starts a loop that will run as long as `i` is less than 5.

RAG+CoI,"Why is Smalltalk sometimes called ""dynamically typed""?","

I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" and not ""untyped"" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.
There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?

,

""Dynamically typed"" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean ""dynamically checked"". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.
See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.

,Smalltalk, and by extension Pharo, is referred to as a ""dynamically typed"" language because it does not require explicit type annotations for variables, parameters, or method returns. Instead, types are associated with objects at runtime rather than at compile time. This means that the type of an object is determined dynamically when the program is executed, rather than being checked statically by the compiler before execution (Pharo By Example, pp. 1, 100).

In Pharo, nearly everything is achieved by sending messages to objects. This dynamic message sending is a core aspect of the language. When a message is sent to an object, the object itself determines how to respond to the message, which is a dynamic operation. This contrasts with statically typed languages where the method to be executed is determined at compile time (Pharo By Example, pp. 100-101).
For example, consider the following Pharo code:

In this code, the message `+` is sent to different objects (an integer and a point), and each object has its own method for responding to the `+` message. The method to be executed is chosen dynamically at runtime based on the receiver of the message (Pharo By Example, p. 100).

The term ""dynamically typed"" is used instead of ""untyped"" because, although there are no explicit type declarations, types still exist and are checked at runtime. The class of an object can be queried using messages like `class` or `isMemberOf:`. For instance:
This shows that objects do have types (their classes), and these types can be inspected dynamically (Pharo By Example, pp. 183-184).

In summary, Smalltalk (and Pharo) is called ""dynamically typed"" because type information is associated with objects at runtime, and type checking occurs dynamically as the program executes. This dynamic nature allows for a flexible and interactive programming environment, which is a hallmark of the Smalltalk family of languages (Pharo By Example, pp. 1, 100-101).",318,,,,,0.5454545454545454,0.6953344572248529,6,"['One of the unique features of Pharo ( and its ancestors ) is that while you are programming , you are living in a world of live objects , not in a world of static program text .', '3 + 4 > > > 7 "" send message + with argument 4 to integer 3 "" ( 1@2 ) + 4 > > > 5@6 "" send message + with argument 4 to point ( 1@2 ) ""', 'Ok , so nearly RAG+CoI,"What is the difference between ""instance Variable Names"" and ""class Variable Names"", in pharo?","

I just created my own package(MyPackage) and class(MyClass) in pharo, using the System Browser.
I understand the terms subclass and category, not getting the meaning of the other two terms (instanceVariableNames, classVariableNames).

,

I have an impression that this question was already asked and answered, but I was not able to find it, probably it was on a mailing list.
Instance variables are just the variables that are personal to an instance, so each instance of the class that you are defining will have it's own set of variables.
Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them ""pool variables"", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. If you have a ""class variable"" in A, you can access the same variable from both instances of A and B. It's like having a global variable for a hierarchy. I recommend not to use this type of variables.
,In Pharo, ""instanceVariableNames"" and ""classVariableNames"" serve distinct purposes and are used to define different types of variables within a class.

Instance variables are defined using the ""instanceVariableNames"" keyword. These variables are private to each instance of the class, meaning that each object created from the class has its own separate copy of these variables. They can be accessed by name within any instance method of the class that defines them, as well as in methods defined in its subclasses (p. 89). However, it is considered bad style in Pharo to access an instance variable directly from a subclass (p. 89). This encapsulation ensures that the internal state of an object is protected and can only be accessed or modified through methods defined in the class.
In this example, each instance of `MyClass` will have its own `name` and `age` variables.

Class variables, defined using the ""classVariableNames"" keyword, are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself. This means that there is only one copy of a class variable, and it is accessible from any instance of the class as well as from the class itself (pp. 109-110). Class variables are useful when you need to maintain a state or data that should be consistent across all instances of a class.
In this example, `SharedCounter` is a class variable that is shared among all instances of `MyClass`.

Class instance variables are similar to instance variables but are defined for the class itself, which is an instance of its metaclass. These variables are private to the class and its subclasses, meaning that each class has its own set of class instance variables, and subclasses inherit these variables but have their own private copies (p. 92). This is different from class variables, which are shared among all instances and the class itself.
In this example, `classInstanceVar` is a class instance variable that is private to `MyClass` and its subclasses.

In summary, instance variables are private to each instance of a class, class variables are shared among all instances and the class itself, and class instance variables are private to the class and its subclasses. Understanding these distinctions is crucial for managing state and behavior in Pharo effectively.",425,,,,,0.5384615384615384,0.7153708989083571,7,"['Class variables are private variables shared by RAG+CoI,"Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?","

Background
In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context).

column3: is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

column1: • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?

,

One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows ""self"". By returning ""self"" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the ""self"" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning ""self"" is the most elegant thing to do.
,In Smalltalk, methods return ""self"" by default if no explicit return value is specified. This design choice is rooted in the language's philosophy and has several practical benefits.

1. **Uniformity and Simplicity**: In Pharo, a dialect of Smalltalk, everything is an object, and objects communicate by sending messages. The pseudo-variable `self` represents the receiver of the message, similar to `this` in Java (Pharo By Example, p. 103). This uniformity simplifies the object model, making it easier to understand and predict the behavior of methods.

2. **Implicit Return of Self**: If a method does not explicitly return a value using the `^` construct, it implicitly returns the receiver (`self`). This can be imagined as if every method without an explicit return ends with `^ self` (Pharo By Example, p. 103). This implicit return ensures that the method always provides a value, maintaining consistency in message passing.

3. **Method Chaining**: One of the practical benefits of returning `self` by default is that it facilitates method chaining. Method chaining allows multiple methods to be called on the same object in a single statement, enhancing code readability and fluency. For example:
Here, `show:` and `cr` are chained together because `Transcript` returns itself after each method call (Pharo By Example, p. 62).

4. **Communication of Intent**: Explicitly returning `self` can sometimes be avoided to communicate that the return value is not of particular interest to the sender. When you explicitly return `self`, you are indicating that the receiver itself is the significant result of the method (Pharo By Example, p. 103).

5. **Dynamic Method Lookup**: Methods in Pharo are dynamically dispatched, meaning that the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver and follows the inheritance chain. This dynamic nature ensures that the correct method is executed based on the actual class of the receiver at runtime (Pharo By Example, pp. 100-101).

In summary, methods return `self` by default in Smalltalk to maintain a simple and uniform object model, facilitate method chaining, and ensure consistent message passing. This design choice aligns with the language's philosophy of everything being an object and all interactions happening through message sends.",361,,,,,0.5714285714285714,0.6922359570697008,8,"['* Method lookup follows the inheritance chain

RAG+CoI,How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set,"

Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.
data:= received data after each refresh.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
""How to check if this data already exist and not add""
How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?

,

Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.
If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:
<pre><code>(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:
This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....

,To check if a number already exists in a set in Pharo and avoid adding it again, you can use the `includes:` method provided by the `Set` class. The `Set` class in Pharo is designed to hold unique elements, meaning it automatically handles duplicates by not adding an element if it already exists in the set. However, explicitly checking for membership before adding can be done using the `includes:` method.
Here is how you can modify your function to check if a number already exists in the set before adding it:
(list < x and: [z includes: list not]) ifTrue: [z add: list].
- `z := Set new.` initializes a new empty set.
- `data := receivedDataAfterEachRefresh.` assumes `data` is the collection of numbers received after each refresh.
- The loop `1 to: length do: [:k | ... ]` iterates over each element in `data`.
- `list := data at: k.` retrieves the k-th element from `data`.
- The condition `(list < x and: [z includes: list not])` checks if the element is less than `x` and not already included in the set `z`.
- If the condition is true, `z add: list` adds the element to the set.
This approach ensures that only unique elements less than `x` are added to the set `z`.
- ""The class Set is a collection which behaves as a mathematical set, i.e., as a collection with no duplicate elements and without any order"" (Pharo By Example, pp. 207-208).
- ""The set operations union, intersection and membership test are implemented by the Collection messages union:, intersection:, and includes:"" (Pharo By Example, p. 208).

,To implement a switch-like structure in Pharo Smalltalk, you can use a series of conditional expressions. Pharo does not have a dedicated switch statement, but you can achieve similar functionality using nested `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages sent to Boolean objects. Here’s how you can refactor your code to make it more readable and maintainable:
1. **Refactor the Command Parsing**: Split the command and its value, then use nested conditionals to handle different commands.
2. **Use the Debugger**: If you need to debug your code, you can insert a `self halt` statement to open the debugger at a specific point in your code.
Here is a refactored version of your code:
1. **Splitting Commands**: The program first checks if the input ends with a newline and then splits the commands based on new lines.
2. **Parsing Each Command**: Each command is split into the command part and the value part.

3. **Conditional Execution**: The command is compared against possible values ('down', 'left', 'right', 'up') using nested `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages. If none of the conditions match, an assertion failure is triggered.
To open the debugger at a specific point, you can insert `self halt` in your code. For example:
ifTrue: [ self halt. turtle down: value ]
When the code execution reaches `self halt`, the debugger will open, allowing you to inspect the state of your program.

- `line := stream nextLine` reads the next line from the file.
- `stream close` closes the stream after reading.

RAG+CoI,Message Not Understood for existing method,"

Ok, so it's like this:
Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?

,

You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.

,In Pharo, when a message is sent to an object, the system first looks for a corresponding method in the object's class. If the method is not found, the lookup continues up the class hierarchy until it reaches the `Object` class. If no method is found by then, the virtual machine causes the object to send itself the message `doesNotUnderstand:` with the original message selector as its argument (Pharo By Example, pp. 101, 107).

The `doesNotUnderstand:` method is implemented in the `Object` class. When invoked, it creates a `MessageNotUnderstood` object and typically starts a debugger in the current execution context (Pharo By Example, pp. 107-108). This mechanism allows developers to intercept such errors and take alternative actions by overriding `doesNotUnderstand:` in any subclass of `Object` (Pharo By Example, pp. 107, 312-313).

In your case, the message `selectors` is understood by the `receiver`, returning `#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)`. However, when you send the message `expression` to the same `receiver`, it throws a `MessageNotUnderstood` error. This indicates that the `expression` method is not implemented in the `receiver`'s class or any of its superclasses.

To fix this issue, you need to ensure that the `expression` method is defined in the class of the `receiver` or one of its superclasses. If the method should exist but is missing, you might need to implement it or check if it is being dynamically loaded or compiled, as sometimes `doesNotUnderstand:` is used to lazily load or compile missing methods (Pharo By Example, p. 322).
Here is an example of how you might define the `expression` method in the `receiver`'s class:
""Implement the method to handle the expression message""

How can you invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo? Do these environments have anything in them like the system() function in certain unix languages to run external shell commands, or the backticks (can't make them here do to the editor, but what you get when you push the key left of ""1"" and above ""TAB"") to capture the output of commands?

,

In Squeak you can use CommandShell, but I don't know what (if anything) is available for Pharo at this time.

,To invoke shell commands from Squeak or Pharo, you can use the `OSProcess` package, which provides functionality similar to the `system()` function in Unix-like languages. This package allows you to run external shell commands and capture their output.
Here is a concise guide on how to do this:
First, you need to ensure that the `OSProcess` package is installed in your Pharo environment. You can do this by using the Monticello Browser to load the package from a repository.
Once the package is installed, you can use it to run shell commands. Here is an example of how to execute a shell command and capture its output:
In this example, the `OSProcess command:` method runs the `ls -l` command (which lists directory contents in Unix-like systems) and captures its output. The result is then printed to the Transcript.
The `OSProcess` package provides methods to capture the standard output and standard error of the executed command. You can access these using the `output` and `error` methods on the result object.
This will display both the standard output and any error messages generated by the command.
If you need to run commands asynchronously, `OSProcess` also supports this. You can start a process and continue with other tasks while it runs.
In this example, the `sleep 10` command runs for 10 seconds, and the script waits for its completion using `waitForExit`.

,To set the day, month, and year values for a `Date` or `DateAndTime` object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak), you can use the class methods provided by these classes. Specifically, you can use `Date class>>fromDays:`, `Date class>>year:month:day:`, and `DateAndTime class>>year:month:day:hour:minute:second:offset:`.

For creating a `DateAndTime` object, you can use the `year:month:day:hour:minute:second:offset:` method. This method allows you to create a new `DateAndTime` by supplying integers for the year, month, day, hour, minute, second, and the time zone offset. Here is an example:
aDateTime := DateAndTime year: 2023 month: 10 day: 5 hour: 14 minute: 30 second: 0 offset: 0.

,

,To check if two arrays contain the same elements in Pharo, you can use the `asSet` method to convert both arrays into sets and then compare these sets for equality. This approach works because sets inherently handle duplicate elements and do not consider the order of elements, which aligns with the requirement of checking for the same elements regardless of order or duplicates.
Here is a concise way to achieve this:
(arr1 asSet = arr2 asSet) ifTrue: [^ true] ifFalse: [^ false].
This code converts both `arr1` and `arr2` to sets and then checks if these sets are equal. If they are equal, it returns `true`
- `asSet` converts an array to a set, which automatically handles duplicates and ignores the order of elements (Pharo By Example, pp. 200-201).
- The `=` operator in Pharo checks for object equality, meaning it will return `true` if the sets contain the same elements (Pharo By Example, p. 182).
(arr1 asSet = arr2 asSet) ifTrue: [^ true] ifFalse: [^ false].
In this example, `arr1` and `arr2` contain the same elements but in different orders. Converting them to sets and comparing will return `true` because sets do not consider the order of elements.

- In Pharo, `=` tests for object equality (whether two objects represent the same value), while `==` tests for object identity (whether two expressions represent the same object) (Pharo By Example, p. 182).
- Sets inherently handle duplicates, so if the arrays contain duplicate elements, they will be considered equal as long as the sets of unique elements are the same (Pharo By Example, p. 200).

,

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.

,Choosing between Squeak and Pharo for a beginning Smalltalker, especially for someone with a background in Ruby, involves understanding the strengths and weaknesses of each environment. Both Squeak and Pharo are implementations of the Smalltalk programming language, but they have different focuses and communities.

1. **Modern and Actively Developed**: Pharo is designed to be a modern, clean, and innovative Smalltalk environment. It does not just replicate the past but reinvents the essence of Smalltalk with evolutionary and incremental changes【4:2†source】【4:9†source】.
2. **Live Coding Environment**: Pharo offers a live coding environment that allows you to modify code while it executes, which can significantly enhance productivity and experimentation【4:8†source】.
3. **Powerful IDE**: Pharo provides a powerful integrated development environment (IDE) with tools to manage complex code and promote good design practices【4:8†source】.

4. **Rich Library and Virtual OS**: Pharo includes a rich library and can be viewed as a virtual operating system, with a fast JITing VM and full access to OS libraries via its Foreign Function Interface (FFI)【4:8†source】.
5. **Community and Culture**: Pharo has a welcoming community that encourages contributions and values improvements. This culture can be very supportive for beginners【4:8†source】.
6. **Portability**: Pharo is highly portable and can run on various platforms including OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, iOS, and Raspberry Pi【4:9†source】.
1. **Learning Curve**: While Pharo is powerful, its modern features and extensive libraries might present a steeper learning curve for absolute beginners【4:6†source】.
2. **Frequent Updates**: The active development and frequent updates might sometimes lead to instability or require frequent learning of new features【4:2†source】.

1. **Educational Focus**: Squeak has a strong focus on educational use and multimedia applications. It is often used in academic settings and for teaching purposes, which might make it more approachable for beginners【4:9†source】.
2. **Rich History and Community**: Squeak has a long history and a large community, which means there is a wealth of resources, tutorials, and community support available【4:9†source】.
3. **Stable and Mature**: Squeak is known for its stability and maturity, making it a reliable choice for learning and experimentation【4:9†source】.

1. **Less Modern**: Compared to Pharo, Squeak might feel less modern and innovative. It tends to stick closer to traditional Smalltalk principles without as many of the modern enhancements found in Pharo【4:9†source】.
2. **User Interface**: The user interface of Squeak might feel outdated compared to the more polished and modern interface of Pharo【4:9†source】.

For a Rubyist starting with Smalltalk, **Pharo** might be the better choice if you are looking for a modern, actively developed environment with a powerful IDE and a supportive community. Its live coding environment and rich libraries can provide a robust platform for learning and experimentation.

On the other hand, if you prefer a more stable and educationally focused environment with a rich history and extensive community resources, **Squeak** could be the way to go. Its stability and maturity make it a reliable choice for beginners.

Ultimately, the choice depends on your specific needs and preferences. If you value modern features and active development, go with Pharo. If you prefer stability and a focus on education, Squeak might be more suitable.",494,,,,,0.3157894736842105,0.6107846795394236,6,"['Pharo does not just copy the past , it reinvents the essence of Smalltalk .', 'The Pharo community is friendly and active .', 'Pharo can run on OS X , Windows , Linux , Android , iOS , and Raspberry Pi .', 'Pharo is a modern , open - source , dynamically typed language supporting live coding inspired by Smalltalk .', 'A rich library that creates an environment so powerful that it can be viewed even as a virtual OS , including a very fast JITing VM and full access to OS libraries and features via its FFI .', 'A live coding environment that allows the programmer to modify code while the code executes , without any need to slow down their workflow .']","['Pharo is highly portable and can run on various platforms including OS X , Windows , Linux , Android , iOS , and Raspberry Pi[(4:9+source )]  .', 'Pharo is designed to be a modern , clean , and innovative Smalltalk environment .', 'Pharo includes a rich library and can be viewed as a virtual operating system , with a fast JITing VM and full access to OS libraries via its Foreign Function Interface ( FFI)[(4:8+source )]  .', 'Pharo offers a live coding environment that allows you to modify code while it executes , which can significantly enhance productivity and experimentation[(4:8+source )]  .', 'Pharo has a welcoming community that encourages contributions and values improvements .', 'It does not just replicate the past but reinvents the essence of Smalltalk with evolutionary and incremental changes[(4:2+source)] [(4:9+source )]  .']",183,3443,783,,,pharo,gpt-4o

RAG (OpenAI),What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?,"

In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

,

The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.
meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.
The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.
Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
which is a little bit more verbose.
To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.
,In Smalltalk, the terms `self` and `yourself` are used within method bodies but serve different purposes.

`self` is a keyword that refers to the object within which the current method is executing. It is known as the ""receiver"" because it is the object that received the message causing the method to execute. This keyword is a pseudo-variable, meaning it cannot be assigned a new value【4:0†source】【4:2†source】.
For example, consider the following method definition:
aStream nextPutAll: self class name, ' new'
In this method, `self` refers to the instance of `Morph` that received the `fullPrintOn:` message【4:9†source】.

`yourself` is a method that simply returns the receiver object. It is often used to ensure that the method returns the receiver, especially in cases where the last message sent in a method might return a different object. This is useful for maintaining a fluent interface or method chaining.
In this case, `yourself` ensures that the method returns `anObject` rather than the result of `initialize`.

- **Purpose**: `self` is used to refer to the receiver within the method body, allowing the method to send messages to itself. `yourself`, on the other hand, is used to return the receiver object explicitly.

- **Usage Context**: `self` is essential for invoking other methods on the same object, ensuring that the correct method is called even in subclasses. `yourself` is typically used at the end of a method to ensure the receiver is returned, which is particularly useful in method chaining.

In summary, while `self` is a pseudo-variable that refers to the receiver of the message, `yourself` is a method that returns the receiver, ensuring the method returns the object itself rather than another result【4:0†source】【4:8†source】.",281,,,,,0.4444444444444444,0.6834458612982264,4,"[""Imagine that we define the following three methods : First we define the method fullPrintOn : on class Morph that just adds to the stream the name of the class followed by the string ' new ' - the idea is that we could execute the resulting string and gets back an instance similar to the receiver ."", 'self is a keyword that refers to the object inside which the current method is executing .', 'We call it "" the receiver "" because this object 60 4.1 Syntactic elements has received the message that caused the method to execute .', 'The Pharo object model Listing 6 - 17 A locally implemented method EllipseMorph > >']","[""fullPrintOn : aStream       aStream nextPutAll : self class name , ' new '   ` ` `   In this method , ` self ` refers to the instance of ` Morph ` that received the ` fullPrintOn : ` message[(4:9+source )]  ."", '# # # ` self `   ` self ` is a keyword that refers to the object within which the current method is executing .', 'It is known as the "" receiver "" because it is the object that received the message causing the method to execute .', 'For example , consider the following method definition :   ` ` ` smalltalk   Morph > >']",120,5969,438,,,pharo,gpt-4o

RAG (OpenAI),Smalltalk initialization variables,"

In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.
How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?
Or is this bad practice and should I always do

,

The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

• We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
• We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
• You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.

The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).
So for a method that, in Java or a similar ""curly bracket language"", might look something like this:
you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:
making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a ""symbol"" in a global dictionary):
That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):
Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.
Getting back to your case, you had this:

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says ""Variable or expression expected"" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.
• #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected
• if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side

• think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well

column1: ,In Pharo, methods that take multiple arguments are defined using keyword messages. Each part of the method name is followed by a colon and an argument name. The correct syntax for defining a method with three arguments in Pharo is as follows:
MethodName: arg1 arg2: arg2Value arg3: arg3Value
^ self var1: arg1 var2: arg2Value var3: arg3Value
Here is a breakdown of the syntax:
- `MethodName:` is the first part of the method name, followed by the first argument `arg1`.
- `arg2:` is the second part of the method name, followed by the second argument `arg2Value`.
- `arg3:` is the third part of the method name, followed by the third argument `arg3Value`.
Each part of the method name is separated by a space, and each argument is specified after its corresponding keyword.
To call this method, you would use the following syntax:
ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: argValue2 var3: argValue3
This syntax ensures that each argument is correctly associated with its corresponding keyword in the method definition.
For example, if you have a class `MyClass` and you want to define a method `setValues: a b: bValue c: cValue`, you would write:
MyClass >> setValues: a b: bValue c: cValue
^ self var1: a var2: bValue var3: cValue
And you would call this method like this:
This approach ensures that the method is correctly defined and can be called without syntax errors【6:6†source】【6:9†source】.",228,,,,,0.0,0.6152203076226794,0,[],[],205,6596,386,,,pharo,gpt-4o

,The error `MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>>Transcript` occurs because the `Transcript` is not a method or property of the `OrderedCollection` class. To print the contents of an `OrderedCollection` to the `Transcript`, you need to iterate over the elements of the collection and send each element to the `Transcript` for display. Here is how you can modify your code to achieve this:
oc do: [:each | Transcript show: each printString

,In Pharo, control constructs such as `if`, `elseif`, and `else` are implemented using message sends to boolean objects with blocks as arguments. Pharo does not have a dedicated syntax for these constructs
column2: instead, it uses messages like `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:`, and `ifTrue:ifFalse:` to achieve conditional logic.

column1: To check multiple conditions using `if`, `elseif`, and `else`, you can chain these messages together. Here is an example of how you can achieve this in Pharo:
ifTrue: [ ""Do something if x is less than 25"" ]
ifTrue: [ ""Do something if x is equal to 25"" ]
ifFalse: [ ""Do something if x is greater than 25"" ]
1. The first condition checks if `x` is less than 25. If true, it executes the corresponding block.
2. If the first condition is false, it checks if `x` is equal to 25. If true, it executes the corresponding block.
3. If both previous conditions are false, it executes the block for `x` being greater than 25.

This approach uses nested `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages to handle multiple conditions, effectively mimicking the `if`, `elseif`, and `else` structure found in other programming languages【4:0†source】【4:1†source】【4:2†source】.",230,,,,,0.4,0.6570725937222258,2,"['For instance , instead of an if - then - else control structure , conditionals are expressed as messages ( such as ifTrue : ) sent to Boolean objects .', 'If the block evaluates to true , execution continues .']","['In Pharo , control constructs such as ` if ` , ` elseif ` , and ` else ` are implemented using message sends to boolean objects with blocks as arguments .', 'If true , it executes the corresponding block .', 'Pharo does not have a dedicated syntax for these constructs

,In Pharo, the `subclassResponsibility` method is used to define abstract methods within abstract classes. An abstract class is designed to be subclassed rather than instantiated directly. It typically contains methods that are meant to be overridden by its subclasses. These methods are placeholders, indicating that the actual implementation should be provided by the subclasses【4:1†source】【4:6†source】.

When you define a method in an abstract class using `self subclassResponsibility`, you are signaling that this method must be implemented by any concrete subclass. If a subclass does not override this method and it gets called, an exception will be raised, indicating that the subclass has not fulfilled its responsibility to provide an implementation【4:1†source】【4:7†source】.
In your case, you have an inheritance hierarchy with `AbstractDictionary` as the abstract superclass. The method `size` in `AbstractArrayDictionary` is defined as follows:

This indicates that `AbstractArrayDictionary` is also an abstract class and that its subclasses (`SimpleDictionary` and `FastDictionary`) must provide their own implementations of the `size` method. This is appropriate because `AbstractArrayDictionary` does not have enough information to provide a meaningful implementation of `size` on its own【4:1†source】.
If you were to implement the `size` method in `AbstractDictionary` as:

this would create an infinite recursive call, as `self size` would call the same method again on the same object, leading to a stack overflow. This is why you should use `self subclassResponsibility` in abstract methods: it clearly indicates that the method must be overridden and prevents such recursive calls【4:1†source】.

Regarding whether to remove the `size` method from `AbstractArrayDictionary`, it depends on whether `AbstractArrayDictionary` itself is intended to be instantiated or if it is purely an abstract class. If it is purely abstract, then it should retain the `self subclassResponsibility` method to ensure that its subclasses implement `size`. If `AbstractArrayDictionary` can be instantiated and should provide a default implementation of `size`, then you would need to provide a concrete implementation instead of using `self subclassResponsibility`【4:1†source】【4:6†source】.

In summary, using `self subclassResponsibility` in abstract methods ensures that subclasses provide necessary implementations, preventing abstract classes from being instantiated without fulfilling their intended design. This approach maintains the integrity of the class hierarchy and ensures that each class provides the necessary functionality.",353,,,,,0.25,0.6247841678311049,3,"['* Return self subclassResponsibility to make a method abstract .', 'a class with an abstract method ( one whose implementation consists of the expression self subclassResponsibility)', 'An abstract class is a class that exists to be subclassed , rather than to be instantiated .']","['In Pharo , the ` subclassResponsibility ` method is used to define abstract methods within abstract classes .', 'When you define a method in an abstract class using ` self subclassResponsibility ` , you are signaling that this method must be implemented by any concrete subclass .', 'An abstract class is designed to be subclassed rather than instantiated directly .', 'In summary , using ` self subclassResponsibility ` in abstract methods ensures that subclasses provide necessary implementations , preventing abstract classes from being instantiated without fulfilling their intended design .']",631,3906,548,,,pharo,gpt-4o
